referenced through narrative clues; Ace's memory holds missing details about their fate; mystery requires player deduction

notes: Déjà Vu NES (1990) is a point-and-click adventure adapted to controller interface. Core world-state communication relies on: (1) memory mechanic as narrative/mechanical danger signal, (2) location-based storytelling replacing dialogue, (3) hardboiled noir aesthetic established through black/white or limited-color NES palette, (4) framing scenario (false murder accusation) creates persistent threat state, (5) boxing background provides mechanical skill expression matching character background. NES version modified memory deterioration mechanics—removed vegetable status timeout but kept Ace's office death trap if visited pre-cure. Environmental danger signals (interrogation tools, corpse, police presence) precede player understanding, forcing investigative behavior. Faction indicators subtle: police as law antagonists, criminal underworld as street threats, player as noir detective protagonist. Chicago 1940s setting communicated through art direction and narrative framing rather than explicit visual markers (due to NES technical limitations).
